locked_facts: Lenz's law: the induced current flows in a direction that opposes the change in flux that caused it
Lenz's law is a consequence of conservation of energy
If flux increases → induced current creates opposing field. If flux decreases → induced current supports the field.
The negative sign in ε = -dΦ/dt represents Lenz's law
Eddy currents are induced currents in bulk conductors (not wires) exposed to changing flux
Eddy currents cause heating (energy loss) — used in induction heating, electromagnetic braking
Eddy currents can be reduced by using laminated cores (thin insulated sheets)
truth_statements: Lenz's law states that the direction of induced current is such that it opposes the change in magnetic flux that produces it
Lenz's law is a direct consequence of conservation of energy — if it aided the change, perpetual motion would result
Eddy currents flow in closed loops within conducting material when the magnetic flux through it changes
Electromagnetic braking uses eddy currents to slow a conductor moving in a magnetic field
Laminated cores reduce eddy current losses by breaking up the current paths

aha_moment.what_to_show: A magnet pushed INTO a coil: flux increases, induced current creates a field that REPELS the magnet (opposing the increase). Magnet pulled AWAY: flux decreases, induced current creates a field that ATTRACTS the magnet (opposing the decrease). The coil always fights the change.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Two animations: push→repel, pull→attract, with induced field arrows showing opposition
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students memorise 'opposes the change' without feeling it — seeing the coil physically resist the magnet in both directions makes Lenz's law intuitive

simulation_states.label: Magnet approaching coil
simulation_states.description: North pole moves toward coil. Flux increases. By Lenz's law, induced current creates a north pole facing the magnet (repulsion). Induced B opposes the increase in flux.
simulation_states.emphasis: Flux increasing → induced field opposes it → coil repels the magnet

simulation_states.label: Magnet receding from coil
simulation_states.description: North pole moves away. Flux decreases. Induced current creates a south pole facing the magnet (attraction). Induced B tries to maintain the flux.
simulation_states.emphasis: Flux decreasing → induced field supports it → coil attracts the magnet

simulation_states.label: Energy conservation proof
simulation_states.description: If Lenz's law were reversed (coil attracted approaching magnet), the magnet would accelerate, inducing more current, attracting harder → infinite energy from nothing. This violates energy conservation.
simulation_states.emphasis: Lenz's law MUST oppose the change — otherwise energy is created from nothing

simulation_states.label: Interactive + eddy currents
simulation_states.description: Student moves magnet. Coil current direction shown. Toggle to eddy current demo: magnet dropped through copper tube falls slowly due to opposing eddy currents. Lamination demo reduces eddy losses.
simulation_states.emphasis: Move the magnet — feel the opposition. Watch eddy currents slow the falling magnet.

common_misconceptions.belief: Lenz's law says induced current opposes the magnetic field
common_misconceptions.correction: Lenz's law says induced current opposes the CHANGE in flux, not the flux itself. If flux is decreasing, the induced current creates a field in the SAME direction as the original field (to maintain the flux).
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show magnet receding — induced field is in the SAME direction as original, supporting the decreasing flux

common_misconceptions.belief: Eddy currents only cause energy loss and are always undesirable
common_misconceptions.correction: Eddy currents have useful applications: electromagnetic braking (trains), induction cooktops, metal detectors, and electromagnetic damping in galvanometers.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show electromagnetic braking — eddy currents usefully slow down a moving conductor
